title: The Best Prime Day Deals on Red Light Therapy Masks and Hair Growth Devices That Actually Work
gist: WIRED guide to Prime Day deals on red light therapy and LED hair growth devices, covering products from $161 to $860. Key signals: CurrentBody LED Hair Growth Helmet at $770 (10% off) showed positive hair regrowth results in WIRED testing including on a balding roommate after 12 weeks, and the Hairmax LaserBand 82 at $679 uses 82 medical-grade lasers and requires only 90-second treatments three times per week.

themes.theme: Consumer Health Tech: Red Light and LED Device Market

insights.claim: CurrentBody's LED Hair Growth Helmet at $770 produced visible new hair growth in WIRED's product tester after 12 weeks, with her hairstylist noting thicker hair—and a male roommate with balding reporting new scalp growth as a secondary test case.
insights.speaker: Boutayna Chokrane
insights.so_what: WIRED editor-tested positive efficacy signals for an $860 consumer LED hair growth device suggest the category is expanding from skeptical early adopters to mainstream wellness buyers, supporting premium pricing in photobiomodulation devices.
insights.quote: After 12 weeks of consistent use, WIRED product tester Molly Higgins spotted new growth along her scalp and sideburns. Her hairstylist commented that her hair felt thicker

insights.claim: Hairmax LaserBand 82 uses 82 medical-grade lasers (not LEDs) requiring only 90-second sessions three times a week, positioning it as a faster alternative to daily-use LED cap competitors in the hair restoration device category.
insights.speaker: Boutayna Chokrane
insights.so_what: Laser-based versus LED-based differentiation in hair restoration devices, combined with session time reduction (90 seconds vs daily sessions), shows the category is competing on compliance convenience as a key purchase driver.
insights.quote: Each treatment takes about 90 seconds, and you only need to use it three times a week. Compared to devices that require daily sessions, that's a much easier routine to stick with.

insights.claim: Laduora's Duo hairbrush device includes serum-filled proprietary pods for scalp treatment, with replacement pods required to continue serum functionality—creating a recurring consumable revenue stream attached to a $300 hardware device.
insights.speaker: Boutayna Chokrane
insights.so_what: Proprietary consumable pods attached to consumer health hardware follows the razor-and-blade model; the device still functions without pods, partially limiting the recurring revenue lock-in but creating a natural upsell path.
insights.quote: The catch is that you'll need to buy replacement pods directly from Laduora if you want to keep using them. But the good news is that the device still works without the serums.
